If you loved this, hit that subscribe, like, and share so we can keep bringing more wild places to your screen!

If these wild places move you, hit subscribe, drop a like, and share it with a fellow nature lover!

comments.author: @IamCarlaC92
comments.text:
I am from Uk and I am dreaming to visit Montana, it’s very pricey to visit but I hope one day
Thanks for sharing this beautiful video

replies.author: @debbiebeck8834
replies.text: I am from Texas and recently visited Montana in May.  It is spectacular.  The majesty of the snow capped mountains and all of the wildlife who are free to roam as they have for thousands of years is so amazing and humbling.  Go, you will not regret it.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks so much! I really hope you get to experience Montana in person one day—it’s every bit as breathtaking as it looks. Wishing you safe travels when that dream finally comes true! If you enjoyed the video, please like, share, and subscribe for more hidden places waiting to be discovered.

comments.author: @douglashento996
comments.text: Glacier  will put you in your place.lil bitty.....

comments.author: @Trumpster71
comments.text: Alaska not Montana is the United States last great wilderness

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That's a fair point, and Alaska is absolutely in a league of its own when it comes to untouched wilderness. We see Montana as one of the last great wilderness areas in the Lower 48, but we appreciate your perspective. Thanks for watching! If you enjoy exploring America's hidden places, please like, subscribe, and share the journey with us.

comments.author: @truehorizons66
comments.text: Watching this makes me want to disconnect from everything and spend a week exploring Montana's wilderness.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That’s exactly the feeling Montana leaves you with. There’s something incredibly peaceful about getting away from the noise and reconnecting with nature. Hope you get to make that trip someday! Thanks for watching—and if you’re enjoying these journeys, please like, subscribe, and share to help us explore more hidden places together.

comments.author: @WhateverIwant-n2l
comments.text: I live in northwest montana and it truly is beautiful and rugged and the chance of getting attacked by a grizzly bear even in town is higher than your chance of getting hit by lightning.  The lakes and rivers are so clean and clear the winters are usually brutal the summers are usually extremely smokey and hot and the cost of living is so high I dont know how we are surviving here

replies.author: @WhateverIwant-n2l
replies.text: I remember one day I was opening the gas station and looked across the street and there was like 3 or 4 deer running through the drive thru across the street and through the parking lot, I liked right off flathead lake and I walked into a deer in the middle of the night walking across my yard and I always had wild turkeys in my yard

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text:
Wow, it really is that beautiful and rugged out there. Crystal clear lakes and rivers, brutal winters, smoky hot summers, and yeah — grizzly bears showing up even near town? That's wild. Sounds like a tough but awesome place to live. Really appreciate you sharing the real story!
If you enjoyed this, hit like, subscribe for more, and share it with your friends!

comments.author: @marybesse7312
comments.text: Montana is beautiful! Glacier National Park is amazing as well

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks so much! Montana truly is one of America's most breathtaking places, and Glacier National Park is absolutely unforgettable. We really appreciate you watching! If you enjoy exploring incredible destinations like this, please consider subscribing, giving the video a like, and sharing it with fellow travel lovers.

comments.author: @A.S.H1np

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical

comments.author: @TenThumbsProductions
comments.text: America is Alaska to Ushuaia and there are plenty of places to get lost in the wilderness. Alaska, the majority of Canada, Wyoming, the Great Basin, west Texas, south east Oregon, Mexico has a ton of great wilderness, Colorado still has some very impressive wilderness, southern and northern Chile, Argentina has a ton, there is only one America and it isn’t a country, friends, go see it all

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Beautifully said. There really are endless wild places across the Americas, and every region has its own story worth discovering. Thanks for sharing your perspective! If you enjoy documentaries like this, don't forget to like, subscribe, and share the channel with fellow travelers.

comments.text:
I WAS WRONG!  PLEASE DISREGARD THE CRITICAL COMMENT BELOW.  I GAVE THIA AWESOME VIDEO A THUMBS UP!

I gave this a video a Thumbs down because at about the 30 minute mark YOU STOPPED naming the towns you describing.  LAME! replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: @Cliffmartin-r5h Thanks for your patience—I checked the script and timeline again, and the location at around 32:51 is Lewistown, Montana, a small town located near the geographic center of the state.

replies.author: @Cliffmartin-r5h
replies.text: ​@TruePlanetOfficalThanks for replying.  We'll, do you recall of the name of that town that you talking about?  Your description of the town starts at about the 30 minute or so mark.  You only say that it is in eastern montana near central montana as I recall.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: I’m truly sorry that this oversight affected your experience with the video. We’ll make sure future videos identify locations more consistently, and we hope you’ll give us another chance to earn your support.

comments.author: @Earthcue
comments.text: The movie "Eat, Pray, Love" was filmed right here in Ubud — and it changed the town forever! After Julia Roberts wandered these streets on screen in 2010, Ubud became a global pilgrimage for spiritual seekers, yoga lovers, and solo travelers looking to find themselves. The real healer from the book, Ketut Liyer, became so famous that he had a waiting list of tourists for years!

replies.author: @MeYouMeYouMeYou6
replies.text: Hollywood is cancer.  Stop idolizing pretentious millionaires

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That’s such a fascinating part of Ubud’s story. It’s amazing how one movie can reshape a place so deeply—do you think Ubud still feels authentic today, or has tourism changed it too much?

comments.author: @dillon-d6b
comments.text: Tourist provide a good source of food for the bears, I like that, tourist are annoying.

replies.author: @ajaxtelamonian5134
replies.text: Its pretty god damn cool. Glacier is stunning. The plains are beautiful. Columbia falls has some nice cabin lodges make sure to check out the Huckleberry hit with the sasquatch statue.

replies.author: @sarahburggraf861
replies.text: I'm jealous. Ohio girl here. Only 🌽 fields and factories 😢

replies.author: @Koali2011
replies.text: It is jaw dropping, tear jerking, awestruck wonderfully BEAUTIFUL!!!! Never been there during the winter season but I’m told it is no joke BRUTAL. Don’t know if I could stand 9 months of it but the summer season will blow you away. It’s beyond words wondrous and the mountains are SO SO massive. No words. And even the best of pictures don’t begin to capture the experience. Go see the mountains!

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Absolutely — Montana really has that rare mix of wide-open beauty and wild nature. Glacier National Park must be incredible in person. What season would you say shows Montana at its very best?

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That sounds amazing — two feet of snow in October is wild! I can see why he loves it. Has he found a favorite spot in Montana yet?

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: It really can feel that untouched, especially once you get away from the main towns and popular spots. The documentary definitely highlights the most dramatic views, but Montana’s wide-open landscapes and wild beauty are very real. Have you ever been there yourself?

replies.author: @mroush6028

replies.author: @BeccaBigSkyMT
replies.text: Yes! Montana it beautiful and wide open spaces. Lots of amazing wildlife, mountains, rivers and lakes, national parks (we also have glacier national Park). Beautiful state to visit but winters are not for the weak 😉

replies.author: @mroush6028

replies.author: @BeccaBigSkyMT
replies.text: ​@TruePlanetOfficalwe always say Montana is full to discourage people from moving here and bringing their "out of state" ways. We welcome you but you will never change Montana or our ways. Come visit but leave the wildlife alone and you will love it

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: @dondavidson3440 I hear you. Some places really do feel like they’re better left quiet and untouched. That kind of feeling says a lot about how special they are.

replies.author: @dondavidson3440

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Couldn't agree more. Some of the best parts of America are the places people drive right past. What's one small town or backroad you'd recommend everyone experience at least once?

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical

replies.author: @dondavidson3440

Get away from the Interstate highways and cites and most people will never see the real America; not just Montana, but the whole middle of the continent!
It is known by the city slickers as fly over country!

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: Montana truly lives up to its nickname as the Big Sky Country. Such breathtaking scenery!

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical

comments.author: @trueglobe66

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical

comments.author: @galenhaugh3158
comments.text: Reintroducing the grizz was the biggest mistake ever!  The policy precludes people from the wilderness, and for what?  So bears can eat elk instead of hunters??

replies.author: @johnnynichols5850

replies.author: @scoobydoo5447

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks for sharing your perspective. It’s definitely a topic that brings out strong opinions, especially for people who spend a lot of time in the outdoors. Have you personally noticed changes in the areas you used to hunt or hike since the grizzlies were reintroduced? I’d genuinely be interested to hear about your experience.
